front: Figure: 37th President of the United States, a California Congressman and Senator before that, and Dwight Eisenhower's Vice President
Figure: this president is the only one in U.S. history to resign from office, doing so amid the Watergate scandal
Figure: this politician was widely nicknamed "Tricky Dick"
Ruler: reportedly pioneered push polling in his 1946 Congressional campaign against Jerry Voorhis by calling Democratic voters and asking if they knew Voorhis was a Communist
work: Early Career: Congress, the Hiss Case, and Red-Baiting Campaigns

front: Ruler: as a Congressman, doggedly pursued accused Soviet spy Alger Hiss
work: Early Career: Congress, the Hiss Case, and Red-Baiting Campaigns

front: Ruler: co-sponsored a bill with Karl Mundt that would have required Communist organizations to register with a "Subversive Activities Control Board"
work: Early Career: Congress, the Hiss Case, and Red-Baiting Campaigns

front: Ruler: won the bitter 1950 California Senate race against Helen Gahagan Douglas by branding her "pink right down to her underwear" on a "Pink Sheet" comparing her voting record to Vito Marcantonio's
work: Early Career: Congress, the Hiss Case, and Red-Baiting Campaigns

front: Ruler: he and that 1950 opponent nicknamed each other "Tricky Dick" and "The Pink Lady"
work: Early Career: Congress, the Hiss Case, and Red-Baiting Campaigns

front: Ruler: his 1950 campaign manager Murray Chotiner colored the "Pink Sheet," and the campaign was caught ripping up a Chinese-language sign alleging a Howard Hughes loan to his brother
work: Early Career: Congress, the Hiss Case, and Red-Baiting Campaigns

front: Ruler: in a famous 1952 televised address, denied receiving $18,000 in unreported gifts and said his wife did not own a mink coat, but vowed to keep the family's gift cocker spaniel rather than resign from Eisenhower's ticket
work: Early Career: Congress, the Hiss Case, and Red-Baiting Campaigns

front: Ruler: in 1958, his car was attacked by a mob while visiting Caracas, Venezuela, shaping his later promotion of Vernon Walters
work: Early Career: Congress, the Hiss Case, and Red-Baiting Campaigns

front: Ruler: gave a 1958 speech about U.S. foreign policy in Montevideo, Uruguay that sparked riots
work: Early Career: Congress, the Hiss Case, and Red-Baiting Campaigns

front: Ruler: was founder-president of the failed frozen orange juice company Citri-Frost
work: Early Career: Congress, the Hiss Case, and Red-Baiting Campaigns

front: Ruler: while at Whittier College, founded the Orthogonian Society (with Albert Upton) for students denied entry to the elite Franklin Society
work: Early Career: Congress, the Hiss Case, and Red-Baiting Campaigns

front: Ruler: lost the 1962 California gubernatorial election to Pat Brown, giving a "last press conference" declaring reporters "won't have Nixon to kick around anymore"
work: Early Career: Congress, the Hiss Case, and Red-Baiting Campaigns

front: Event: this 1959 exchange between Vice President Nixon and Soviet Premier Nikita Khrushchev, staged in a model kitchen at the American National Exhibition in Moscow, saw Khrushchev sample Pepsi
work: The Vice Presidency, the Kitchen Debate, and the 1960 Election

front: Event: in these 1960 events, radio listeners judged Nixon the winner while television viewers preferred the more telegenic John F. Kennedy
work: The Vice Presidency, the Kitchen Debate, and the 1960 Election

front: Event: before the first of these events, Nixon injured his knee on a car door, leading to a hospital stay and infection that, with weight loss and a five-o'clock shadow, left him looking haggard on camera
work: The Vice Presidency, the Kitchen Debate, and the 1960 Election

front: Event: the third of these 1960 debates focused on the Taiwanese-controlled islands of Quemoy and Matsu
work: The Vice Presidency, the Kitchen Debate, and the 1960 Election

front: Ruler: as a freshman House representative, shared a sleeping car with fellow representative John F. Kennedy on a train back to Washington after debating him in McKeesport, Pennsylvania over the Taft-Hartley Act
work: The Vice Presidency, the Kitchen Debate, and the 1960 Election

front: Ruler: signed the Endangered Species Act and created the Environmental Protection Agency, partly inspired by the first Earth Day and teach-ins backed by the UAW
Ruler: Congress overrode his veto of the Clean Air Act (championed by Edmund Muskie) and his veto of the Clean Water Act (motivated by the Cuyahoga River Fire)
Ruler: his administration temporarily banned offshore drilling in federal waters after the Santa Barbara oil spill, and his Interior Secretary Walter Hickel tightened offshore drilling regulations
Ruler: proposed the Family Assistance Plan, a guaranteed-income measure that would have given a family of four $1,600 per year; it passed the House but failed in the Senate
Ruler: endorsed the Equal Rights Amendment after Martha Griffiths reintroduced it to Congress
Ruler: imposed wage-price controls that capped beef prices but not corn and wheat cattle feed prices, causing a nationwide beef shortage
Ruler: took the United States off the gold standard as part of his namesake economic "Shock," and modified exchange rates in the Smithsonian Agreement
Ruler: during his presidency, the Soviets secretly bought $750 million in U.S. crops in the "Great Grain Robbery," and a uranium cartel raised uranium's price from $6 to $41 per pound
Ruler: instituted a national speed limit in response to the 1973 oil crisis
Ruler: his "New Federalism" policy shifted power to the states through general revenue sharing and non-earmarked block grants
Ruler: appointed conservative commentator William F. Buckley Jr. as a UN delegate, and was president during the Apollo 11 Moon landing

Event: this 1972 trip made Nixon the first sitting U.S. president to visit China; he met Mao Zedong and shook hands with Zhou Enlai, and it was arranged through Henry Kissinger's secret diplomacy and preceded by "ping-pong diplomacy"

Event: journalist Max Frankel won a Pulitzer for his coverage of this trip, during which aide Winston Lord was cropped out of photographs of a meeting involving Pakistani leader Yahya Khan's government
Event: this 1972 diplomatic document, an example of Henry Kissinger's "constructive ambiguity," was the first of the "Three Communiques" and began normalizing U.S.-China relations
Event: one politician called Nixon's 1972 China trip the "week that changed the world"; Nixon was accompanied by Secretary of State William Rogers
Event: as president-elect in 1968, Nixon secretly used Anna Chennault as an intermediary to urge South Vietnamese President Nguyen Van Thieu to stall the Paris peace talks until after the election
back: Nixon's Sabotage of the 1968 Paris Peace Talks

front: Ruler: in a 1969 speech, appealed to the "Silent Majority" while outlining his policy of "Vietnamization," increasing the role of South Vietnamese forces and American helicopters
Ruler: ordered the 1970 invasion of Cambodia, targeting Viet Cong hideouts there
Event: in the early hours of May 9, 1970, Nixon impulsively visited the Lincoln Memorial to speak with student protesters, worrying his bodyguards
back: Nixon's 1970 Visit to the Lincoln Memorial

front: Ruler: a massive May 1971 demonstration in Washington, D.C. protesting his Vietnam policy resulted in over 12,000 arrests, the largest mass arrest in U.S. history
Ruler: ordered Operation Giant Lance based on his self-described "madman theory," as he related to Chief of Staff H.R. Haldeman
Event: this scandal stemmed from a break-in at the Democratic National Committee headquarters; the "Smoking Gun" tape captured Nixon discussing a cover-up with Chief of Staff H.R. Haldeman

Event: this compilation, revealed by White House Counsel John Dean, targeted critics including columnist Jack Anderson, historian Arthur Schlesinger Jr., journalist Daniel Schorr, and actor Paul Newman
Ruler: cartoonist Herblock repeatedly depicted this president climbing out of a sewer

Event: in this episode, Nixon had special prosecutor Archibald Cox fired, prompting Attorney General Elliot Richardson and Deputy AG William Ruckelshaus to resign rather than comply; Nixon then declared "I am not a crook" at a press conference at Disney's Contemporary Resort

Event: Nixon had earlier offered to let the nearly deaf Senator John Stennis listen to and summarize the tapes for the special prosecutor; Cox's refusal of that compromise directly led to this episode
Event: this Supreme Court case, presided over by Chief Justice Warren Burger, forced Nixon to surrender sixty-four taped conversations to new special prosecutor Leon Jaworski

Event: these recordings contained a notorious 18.5-minute gap, blamed on secretary Rose Mary Woods's "Rose Mary Stretch," and had profanity replaced in transcripts with "expletive deleted"; they are now held in Yorba Linda, California
Figure: this Nixon Chief of Staff described himself as Nixon's "S.O.B." and spent 18 months in prison after resigning in 1973
Ruler: resigned the presidency in August 1974 rather than face impeachment over the Watergate scandal, becoming the only U.S. president ever to resign

Event: Gerald Ford issued this proclamation (officially Proclamation 4311), absolving Nixon of all offenses committed between January 20, 1969 and August 9, 1974, in the name of preserving national "tranquility"
Event: this action was announced alongside an amnesty program for Vietnam War draft dodgers, and led press secretary Jerald terHorst to resign in protest
Event: critics called this action a modern-day "corrupt bargain," and it was a main reason Gerald Ford lost the 1976 election to Jimmy Carter

Work: this 1987 minimalist opera by John Adams, with libretto by Alice Goodman and originally directed by Peter Sellars, opens with the landing of the plane the Spirit of '76 and dramatizes Nixon's 1972 China trip

Figure: in Nixon in China, this First Lady sings the aria "This is prophetic" at the Summer Palace after receiving a glass elephant figurine, and rushes onstage during a ballet to help a peasant girl she believes is being whipped to death
Work: this opera's orchestral intermezzo "The Chairman Dances" depicts Mao Zedong and his wife dancing, and the opera ends with Zhou Enlai asking "How much of what we did was good?"
Figure: this First Lady personally aided relief efforts after the 1970 Great Peruvian Earthquake
Work: this novel's final section is epigraphed by Nixon's one-word question "What?," and fictionalizes him as a theater night manager named "Richard M. Zhlubb"
Work: in this 2022 story collection, a girl named Tricia writes a letter to Nixon addressed to him "in hell"
Work: in this novel, Nixon narrates the story, urges a crowd to "drop your pants for America," and is sodomized by Uncle Sam amid the fictionalized execution of the Rosenbergs in Times Square
Work: Nixon appears as the character "Starbuck" in this Kurt Vonnegut novel
Work: in this graphic novel, Nixon serves five straight presidential terms after Doctor Manhattan wins him the Vietnam War
Work: this Rick Perlstein biography uses Nixon's Orthogonian-Franklin college rivalry to argue he hated elites
Work: in this book, historian Garry Wills called Nixon "the last liberal"
Ruler: on Rowan & Martin's Laugh-In in 1968, said the show's catchphrase "Sock it to me?" in an attempt to appeal to younger voters
Concept: rubber masks depicting this president's face were worn by the bank-robbing surfers in the poster for the film Point Break
Concept: psychiatrist John E. Fryer wore one of these objects to conceal his identity while testifying at a 1972 APA meeting on removing homosexuality from the DSM
Institution: this complex in Yorba Linda, California features a replica of the Oval Office; Duke, San Clemente, and Fullerton all rejected offers to host it
back: Richard Nixon Presidential Library and Museum
